differences.title: 1. PURPOSE
differences.leftDesc: Optimized backend for specific frontend needs, improving performance and developer experience.
differences.rightDesc: Single backend serves all frontends, simplifying initial setup but potentially causing issues.

differences.title: 2. TEAM STRUCTURE
differences.leftDesc: Frontend teams manage their BFF, enabling faster iteration and alignment with UI requirements.
differences.rightDesc: Backend teams manage the main API, requiring close collaboration and potential bottlenecks.

differences.title: 3. COMPLEXITY
differences.leftDesc: More backend services to manage, increasing operational overhead but isolating complexity.
differences.rightDesc: Fewer backend services, simplifying deployment but potentially leading to monolithic concerns.

differences.title: 4. PERFORMANCE
differences.leftDesc: Fetches only necessary data, reducing payload size and improving frontend load times.
differences.rightDesc: May fetch excess data, increasing payload size and potentially impacting performance.

differences.title: 5. DEVELOPMENT VELOCITY
differences.leftDesc: Frontend teams can deploy independently, leading to quicker feature delivery and experimentation.
differences.rightDesc: Changes require backend coordination, potentially slowing down frontend development cycles.

differences.title: 6. REUSABILITY
differences.leftDesc: APIs are specific to a frontend, limiting reuse across different clients or platforms.
differences.rightDesc: APIs are designed for general use, promoting code sharing and consistency.

differences.title: 7. MAINTAINABILITY
differences.leftDesc: Each BFF handles its own logic, making it easier to update or refactor without affecting others.
differences.rightDesc: Maintaining a single API for all can become complex and difficult to manage over time.
